content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

September 9, 1992

Chris M. Maupin
Political Consultant
2665 Mateson Way
Sacramento, CA

Re: Your Request For Advice
Our File No. I-92-389

Dear Mr. Maupin:

You have requested advice regarding the slate mailer organization provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1/ Because your request is a general inquiry, we treat it as one for informal assistance pursuant to Regulation 18329(c).2/

QUESTIONS

1. May a slate mailer organization receive contributions in addition to the payments it receives from candidates and ballot measure committees who wish to appear in a slate mailer?

2. May an elected officeholder serve on the committee that determines who will be endorsed in a slate mailer?

3. May a political consulting firm which is a sole proprietorship and whose owner serves as a member of the slate mailer organization's endorsement committee, be paid by the organization for management services?

4. May a slate mailer organization include in the "Notice to Voters" required by Section 84305.5 additional information concerning the organization so long as the original requirements of Section 84305.5(a)(2) have also been met?

___________________________

1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2/ Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c).)

428 J Street, Suite 800 ● P.O. Box 807 ● Sacramento, CA 95812 ● (916) 322-5660

Chris M. Maupin
Page Two
I-92-389


     5.  Must all of the persons who serve on the slate mailer
organization's endorsement committee be listed as officers of the
slate mailer organization?

                              CONCLUSIONS

     1.  The slate mailer organization may receive contributions.
However, receipt of contributions totaling $1,000 or more in a
calendar year will require the organization to file recipient
committee campaign statements in addition to its slate mailer
organization campaign statements.

     2.  The Act does not prohibit an officeholder from serving on
a committee that determines who will be included in a slate
mailer.  However, if the slate mailer organization also qualifies
as a recipient committee, it may become a "controlled" committee.

     3.  The Act does not prohibit a slate mailer organization
from paying a consultant for management services.

     4.  The "Notice to Voters" language required under Section
84305.5(a)(2) must be kept separate from any other information.

     5.  All persons who have final decision making authority as
to which candidates or measures will be supported or opposed in
the organization's slate mailers must be listed on the
organization's statement of organization (Form 401).

                                 FACTS

     Hands On Campaign Consulting is considering producing a slate
card for distribution in the greater Sacramento area.  The firm
would like to assemble a group of individuals to discuss which
campaign to approach for placement on and payment for the slate
card.  The card would include detailed information on who served
on this committee and what criteria they used in making their
decisions.

                               ANALYSIS

     1. and 2.  Section 82048.3 defines "slate mailer
organization" as any person who, directly or indirectly, does all
of the following:

          (1) Is involved in the production of one or more slate
     mailers and exercises control over the selection of the
     candidates and measures to be supported or opposed in the
     slate mailers.

Chris M. Maupin
I-92-389
Page Three

     (2) Receives or is promised payments totaling five hundred
dollars or more in a calendar year for the production of one
or more slate mailers.

     Generally, payments received by a slate mailer organization
are not "contributions" because it is presumed that appearance in
slate mailers constitutes "full and adequate consideration." However, when a slate mailer organization receives an unearmarked
payment to help defray the general cost of publishing a slate
mailer, no such consideration is provided in exchange for the
payment and such payments meet the definition of "contribution"
under Section 82015. (Finley Advice Letter, No. A-92-219.3/) Any
person receiving contributions totaling $1,000 or more in a
calendar year qualifies as a "committee" under Section 82013(a)
and must file recipient committee campaign statements.

     A slate mailer organization does not include a candidate or
officeholder or their controlled committees, an official committee
of any political party, a legislative caucus committee or a
committee primarily formed to support or oppose a candidate,
officeholder or ballot measure. (Section 82048.4(b).)

     If the organization qualifies as a recipient committee and a
candidate or officeholder serves on the organization's endorsement
committee, the recipient committee will become a "controlled"
committee. Section 82016 provides:

     "Controlled committee" means a committee which is controlled
     directly or indirectly by a candidate or state measure
     proponent or which acts jointly with a candidate, controlled
     committee or state measure proponent in connection with the
     making of expenditures. A candidate or state measure
     proponent controls a committee if he, his agent or any other
     committee he controls has a significant influence on the
     actions or decisions of the committee.

     The Commission has advised that a candidate or elected
officer who is a voting member of a committee's board of directors
is presumed to be a controlling candidate, since the candidate or
officeholder probably exercises "significant influence on the
actions or decisions of the committee." (Ferguson Advice Letter,
No. A-86-044.) An officeholder who participates in decisions
concerning candidates and measures to be included in a committee's
slate mailers would also be presumed to exercise significant
influence on the actions or decisions of the committee.


3/   Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available
in many law libraries. Alternatively, copies of these materials
and Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission
at a cost of 10¢ per page.

Chris Maupin
I-92-389
Page Four


     You should also note that the Act contains certain
restrictions on candidates and officeholders with regard to
committees which they control. If the organization will qualify
as a recipient committee, any officeholder who wishes to serve on
the endorsement committee should contact the Commission to discuss
these restrictions.

     3. The Act does not restrict the types of payments which can
be made by slate mailer organizations. However, recipient
committees are subject to the Act's restrictions on use of
campaign funds. (Section 89510, et seq.) Section 89513(b)
provides that campaign funds may be used to pay for professional
services if the services are directly related to a political,
legislative, or governmental purpose. Payment by a recipient
committee for management services provided by a political
consultant would be considered directly related to a political
purpose.

     4. Section 84305.5 requires that slate mailers which are
produced by slate mailer organizations contain certain specified
information, including the name and address of the slate mailer
organization. The statute also requires that a "Notice to Voters"
be included in the slate mailer and specifies the language to be
included in the notice. The notice must be set apart from all
other printed matter and placed in a box. The Act does not allow
for additional information to be included in the "Notice to
Voters". (Lowenstein Advice Letter, No. A-88-186.)

     5. Section 84108(a)(3) specifies the information required to
be included in the statement of organization filed by a slate
mailer organization, and requires disclosure of the full name,
street address, and telephone number of each person with final
decision making authority as to which candidates or measures will
be supported or opposed in the organization's slate mailers.
Therefore, if any of the individuals chosen to serve on the
endorsement committee have final decision making authority, they
must be listed in the statement of organization.

     If you have any additional questions, please contact me at
(916) 322-5662.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Scott Hallabrin
                                        Acting General Counsel

                                        [signature]

                                        By: Linda K. Moureaux
                                            Political Reform Consultant
                                            Technical Assistance and
                                            Analysis Division

Chris N.
